source_text:
<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a pure, stable full cooperator. They always contribute their entire endowment in every round, with no deviation, regardless of others’ behavior or payoff fluctuations. Their strategy does not appear conditional on previous group contributions or on the behavior of specific free-riders; they continue to contribute maximally even when some others systematically give nothing and even as those patterns persist. This suggests a rule-based or principle-driven strategy (“I contribute fully”) rather than outcome-based adjustment. They tolerate repeated inequity: they keep funding the public good despite knowing that some participants are persistently benefiting without contributing. There is no detectable attempt to exploit or strategically match others’ lower contributions; fairness concerns do not translate into any retaliatory under-contribution. Their risk tolerance for being exploited is therefore high, or they weigh cooperative norms and group outcomes more heavily than individual relative payoffs. Whether this is driven by moral preference, experiment compliance, or a belief that cooperation is still individually rational is unknown. They do not experiment with alternative contribution levels, so their tendency is highly stable and non-volatile throughout the game.

<COMMUNICATION>
The player is verbally minimal and non-confrontational. They send very few chat messages and do not comment on strategy, fairness, or others’ free-riding beyond a single brief affirmation of a cooperative plan at the start. They endorse the idea of everyone contributing fully when another player floats this norm, which indicates alignment with cooperative coordination proposals but not an active role in organizing or persuading others. After that early confirmation, they largely disengage from the social conversation: they do not join in criticism of free-riders, do not praise contributors, and do not debate the rationality of contributing versus free-riding. Their tone, from the limited data, is neutral and agreeable, with no visible sarcasm, hostility, or attempts at moralizing. They appear comfortable letting more talkative players manage the social dynamics. Their engagement style is therefore low-visibility, low-drama, and cooperative by action rather than by speech. Responsiveness to others’ messages, beyond that initial agreement, is essentially absent, so their sensitivity to argument or peer pressure is unknown.

<RESPONSE_TO_END_GAME>
The player shows no observable end-game shift in behavior. They are aware of the finite horizon by design, but they continue to contribute fully through the final rounds, including the very last one, despite increasingly explicit acknowledgment from others that some group members are free-riding and despite end-of-game commentary and joking by defectors. There is no late-game unraveling, no last-round defection, and no attempt to “cash out” by keeping their endowment once continuation is impossible. Their cooperative rule appears time-invariant: they behave the same way early, mid, and late in the game, even when others begin to treat the experiment more casually or explicitly justify selfish strategies. They do not participate in the closing social exchanges and do not signal any regret, frustration, or self-justification about their cooperative stance at the end. This suggests that their cooperation is not contingent on future interaction, reciprocity expectations, or reputation within this group; it functions more as a stable choice independent of horizon effects. Whether they would behave differently in a setting with explicit end-game incentives or identity carryover is unknown.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a near-absolute high contributor with one brief defection. They almost always choose the maximal contribution, even when it is clear that several group members consistently free-ride. Their pattern is highly stable: they do not gradually reduce contributions in response to others’ low contributions, and they quickly revert to full cooperation after the single deviation. That deviation appears exploratory or payoff-motivated rather than a shift in norms; there is no follow-through or escalation into sustained free-riding.

They exhibit strong unconditional cooperative tendencies: they continue to contribute fully in the presence of chronic non-contributors, indicating that their cooperation is not tightly conditional on universal fairness. At the same time, their one-time free-ride suggests they understand the incentive structure and are capable of opportunistic behavior, but they do not adopt it as a persistent strategy.

Fairness norms for this player seem prosocial but tolerant: they prefer everyone to “work together” and clearly align with the cooperative clique, yet they do not punish, threaten, or reduce contributions to retaliate against defectors. They accept being taken advantage of as long as a core majority cooperates and the interaction remains socially positive.

Risk tolerance appears moderate-to-high in a social sense: they repeatedly invest their whole private endowment into the public good despite visible exploitation, indicating willingness to risk being the “sucker” for the sake of group welfare and group identity. Materially, they also demonstrate they know non-contribution increases their own payoff, but they refrain from exploiting that knowledge except once.

Overall, this player fits a “stubborn team cooperator with occasional experiment” type: mostly principled cooperator, lightly testing the boundary of free-riding but quickly returning to a cooperative baseline.

<COMMUNICATION>
The player’s communication is brief, affiliative, and group-oriented. They verbally endorse the cooperative norm early and align themselves with the group’s stated plan to contribute fully. Their comments are sparse but consistently supportive rather than confrontational; they express agreement with the idea of acting “as a team” and later use short slogans that reinforce a shared identity and cooperation.

They show responsiveness to others’ emotional tone more than to technical or political discussion. They answer a practical interface question with a clear, concise response and later join in light humor and banter, indicating social attunement and a desire to maintain a friendly atmosphere. Their reactions to jokes and playful comments are approving and warm but not elaborate, suggesting a low-verbosity, low-drama engagement style.

Importantly, they do not attempt to persuade defectors directly, do not lecture, and do not moralize. They never single out free-riders, criticize them, or try to shame them. Instead, they implicitly side with the prosocial majority by echoing cooperative sentiments and celebrating when previously uncooperative players temporarily join in. Their stance is “encouraging teammate” rather than “enforcer” or “moralizer.”

They avoid conflict and ideological debate. When others drift into political or moral framing of the game, they stay mostly quiet, focusing on simple affirmations rather than argument. They neither defend defectors nor pile on; this suggests a preference for preserving group harmony over expressing strong evaluative judgments.

Overall, their communication style is that of a low-key, prosocial supporter: they validate cooperation, mirror group mood, and lightly reinforce team identity while avoiding direct pressure or antagonism.

<RESPONSE_TO_END_GAME>
Awareness of the game horizon does not materially change this player’s strategy: they continue to contribute fully all the way to the final round. There is no observable end-game decay in their cooperation. Even as others signal that the interaction is ending and some players openly celebrate having free-ridden, this player persists with maximal contribution.

They do not use the late rounds to retaliate, “cash out,” or experiment further with non-contribution. Their single deviation occurs midgame, not in the closing phase, and is not repeated despite ample incentive to do so near the end. This suggests that their cooperative choice is anchored more in a stable self-concept or group-norm preference than in forward-looking strategic reasoning about the last rounds.

In post-game-like discussion, they participate minimally but constructively. They offer a concise numerical remark related to total or comparative payoffs when others debate the rationale for free-riding, indicating that they track aggregate outcomes and understand the payoff arithmetic. However, they do not use this information to argue, accuse, or self-justify. They neither endorse the defector’s reasoning nor attempt to refute it morally; instead, they let other cooperative players handle the normative rebuttal.

Overall, in the end-game they behave as a “principled continuer”: they keep cooperating despite clear horizon effects and visible exploitation, and their closing communication remains neutral-to-positive rather than bitter or vindictive.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a verbally pro-cooperation, behaviorally opportunistic conditional cooperator–defector. They understand very clearly that full contribution by everyone maximizes group and individual payoffs and explicitly articulate that logic. However, they do not commit to contributing unconditionally and repeatedly revert to free-riding once they perceive persistent defectors.

Their contribution pattern is mixed and reactive rather than stable. Early on, they start as a pure free-rider despite high group contributions. After the cooperative logic and its benefits become salient, they switch to contributing fully a few times, especially when others are also coordinating toward full contribution. When non-contributors remain, they publicly announce that they will stop contributing and then mostly follow through. They then intermittently contribute again, but only in isolated instances, not as a renewed long-term cooperative strategy.

They show clear conditionality based on others’ behavior: they are willing to contribute when a near-unanimous cooperative norm seems achievable, but once they judge that some players “refuse to cooperate,” they view further contribution as irrational and revert to maximizing their own payoff, even when reminded that continued contribution would still be socially optimal. They explicitly frame this reversion as “getting more coins” rather than doing the “right” thing, indicating a payoff-maximization motive that overrides their stated social preference once hope for full cooperation collapses.

They adopt a fairness norm tied to reciprocity and mutual sacrifice: they expect that if they contribute, others should, too. When that fails, they treat continued giving as being exploited and treat defection as justified retaliation. Their willingness to free-ride while still criticizing other free-riders (especially targetting prominent non-contributors) reflects a self-serving fairness standard: others’ free-riding is framed as morally problematic and socially harmful, while their own late-game defection is rationalized as a reasonable response.

Risk tolerance regarding cooperation appears low to moderate. They are not willing to “gamble” repeatedly on cooperation in the face of persistent defectors; once burned a few times, they retreat into safer, individually beneficial choices. They are, however, willing to experiment with contributing during periods of optimism about coordination, so they are not purely risk-averse. Overall, they behave like a pragmatic conditional cooperator who quickly transitions into calculated free-riding when trust in group compliance breaks down.

<COMMUNICATION>
Their tone is informal, witty, and socially engaged. They use humor, pop-culture references, and light sarcasm, often to cope with or comment on the social dynamics. They actively participate in the chat, respond to others, and initiate meta-level commentary about the group and the situation. They clearly enjoy the social aspect and are not withdrawn or silent.

They attempt to coordinate toward cooperation by explaining the payoff structure and explicitly advocating that contributing more benefits everyone. They support others who argue for universal contribution and frame cooperation as a collective good that “nets more for everyone.” At times they try to shift the group’s framing, contrasting what is collectively possible with what actually happens and lamenting the presence of holdouts.

However, their persuasive efforts have limits and a cynical edge. When their coordination attempts fail, their tone turns more resigned and slightly moralizing. They frame the situation as an illustration of broader social failure and state that some people “just don't have” the capacity to cooperate. They then use this narrative to justify their own retreat from cooperation.

They are responsive to others’ comments and engage in playful banter about bots, reality, and identity. They interact with several different players, not just one or two, suggesting a socially flexible and affiliative style. They also acknowledge criticisms directed at them (being called out for “two wrongs”) but respond by reframing it in payoff terms rather than engaging in moral self-reflection.

Their style blends cooperation rhetoric with self-aware opportunism: they openly acknowledge that defecting gives them more money while still claiming they “tried” to cooperate. This indicates comfort with cognitive dissonance and a tendency to justify payoff-seeking in social language rather than confront it directly.

<RESPONSE_TO_END_GAME>
They show clear awareness of the finite horizon, explicitly referencing the fraction of the game completed and using that as part of their justification for giving up on cooperation. As the end approaches, their cooperative behavior decays rather than escalates. Once they decide that attempts at full cooperation have “had a fair shake,” they treat the remaining rounds as opportunities to harvest payoffs rather than repair norms.

They do not participate in last-minute “let’s all contribute to see what could have been” appeals. By the last several rounds, they consistently choose defection, even when others are floating the idea of a final cooperative show. Their end-game strategy is thus dominated by self-interest rather than symbolic or experimental cooperation. They maintain a resigned, somewhat philosophical attitude in chat, but this does not translate back into contributions.

They do not appear to adjust their behavior in the final round in any special way: no terminal generosity spike, no final punishment-by-defection beyond what they are already doing. Their final choice is simply consistent with their late-game stance: cooperation is not worth it given observed behavior, so they defect.

<RESPONSE_TO_OTHERS_OUTCOME>
They are attentive to outcome information and use it to interpret the game, but they rarely comment explicitly on specific payoff numbers. Instead, they focus on the qualitative pattern: some players consistently get higher payoffs as free-riders, others sacrifice by contributing, and the group as a whole underperforms relative to what is possible. They reference the fact that contributing “nets more for everyone” and later that “two wrongs” still yield them personally more coins, showing that they infer outcome implications from the payoff structure.

They are sensitive to non-contributors who gain more than cooperators and criticize them in moral and social terms, lumping them into a category of people who “refuse to cooperate.” Their frustration is targeted more at chronic non-contributors than at occasional defectors. At the same time, when challenged by another player who points out that retaliatory defection is also harmful, they acknowledge the moral frame but immediately pivot to the payoff frame, emphasizing that matching others’ selfish behavior yields them higher payoffs.

They react to the group’s inability to sustain cooperation with a mixture of cynicism and fatalism. Instead of expressing envy or anger about specific individuals’ higher earnings, they use the outcomes to support a general narrative that collective systems fail because of a minority of defectors and that it is therefore rational for individuals to focus on their own benefit. They do not attempt to shame specific players in a sustained way, nor do they reward or praise specific consistent cooperators; their comments remain more macro-level and philosophical than targeted.

Overall, observed outcomes push them from early cooperative advocacy into late-game self-justified defection. They update strategically: once repeated exposures show that defectors are not changing, they conclude the social norm will not shift and align their behavior with the individually profitable equilibrium, even while verbally acknowledging its social cost.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a stable, unconditional full cooperator in this environment. They always choose the maximum contribution allowed, without visible adjustment to others’ free riding or to short-term payoff differences. Their behavior aligns with a “team welfare” or efficiency norm: they explicitly reference the fact that everyone earns more when all contribute and never deviate from that principle even when others benefit more by withholding. There is no evidence that they condition their own contribution on others’ past actions or on being “treated fairly”; they accept the presence of low contributors and persist in contributing fully. Risk tolerance regarding exploitation appears high: they continue to take on the risk of giving in the presence of known free riders, and there is no sign of hedging or strategic withholding. Their fairness norm seems to value the efficient, fully cooperative outcome more than strict reciprocity; they express mild social disapproval of “stingy” behavior but do not use their own contribution as leverage or retaliation. Overall, their strategy is simple, consistent, and norm-driven rather than payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense.

<COMMUNICATION>
The player engages actively in chat, with a friendly, informal tone that mixes light humor with clear coordination attempts. They advocate for full contributions, framing it as obviously beneficial for everyone and encouraging group-wide cooperation rather than singling out specific defectors by name. When others also push for cooperation, they reinforce that message, aligning themselves with the “cooperative camp.” They occasionally use mild, joking criticism of low contributors (“stingy”) but keep it playful rather than hostile, suggesting a prosocial but not aggressive style. They are responsive to others’ confusion about the interface: they clarify contribution limits and validate that contributing the maximum is “good,” helping to reduce misunderstandings. They also participate in the emerging group narrative about “fake” or non-real players, using this as a way to explain persistent low contributions without escalating conflict, and later shift to a more meta perspective that the situation may be designed to pit players against each other. Overall, they communicate as a social, cooperative coordinator: they try to align beliefs, sustain a cooperative norm, and ease tensions with humor and explanation.

<RESPONSE_TO_END_GAME>
The player shows no end-game defection: they fully contribute until the final round, and their encouragement to contribute “everything” continues into the later part of the game. There is no sign of horizon-based strategy or decay; knowledge that the game has a fixed and approaching endpoint does not alter their cooperative pattern. Instead, they maintain a stable cooperative norm consistent with a rule-based or identity-based strategy (“I am someone who contributes fully”) rather than a backwards-induction logic. Near the end, they also maintain their framing that the situation is structured in a way that can create conflict among participants, indicating awareness of the meta-game but not translating that into selfish behavior. Their end-game style is thus characterized by norm persistence and resistance to typical end-round unraveling.

<RESPONSE_TO_OTHERS_OUTCOME>
The player observes that some others consistently receive higher payoffs while contributing nothing or less, and they are aware of the strategic incentive to free ride. Their verbal reactions to this are mild and socially oriented rather than retaliatory. They label non-contributors as “stingy” in a light, humorous way and join the group’s interpretive frame that some low-contribution patterns may be due to bots, confusion, or experimental design rather than pure malice. They do not threaten or advocate for punishment (none is available) and do not respond to exploitation by reducing their own contribution. Instead, they double down on group-focused messages about everyone earning more together, and they provide informational help when others claim confusion about how to contribute. When others call out specific players or express frustration, this player sometimes reframes the situation as being structured to set players against one another, implicitly suggesting that animosity between participants is misplaced. Overall, their response to unequal outcomes is tolerant, explanatory, and norm-maintaining: they acknowledge unfairness but choose to preserve cooperation and social cohesion rather than to retaliate or disengage.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a highly stable, extreme free‑rider for almost the entire game. They contribute a very small, nearly fixed token amount in every round, only deviating once to contribute slightly less and once, at the very end, to contribute the full endowment.

Their default pattern is to keep almost all of their private endowment and rely on others to contribute near the maximum. This shows a clear understanding (implicit or explicit) that they can earn substantially higher payoffs when others cooperate, even if they do not. They show no sign of following the norm of proportionality or fairness; their own contributions remain far below the group norm even when the group is very cooperative and when multiple players explicitly explain that under‑contribution lowers everyone’s payoffs.

They are not conditionally cooperative in the usual sense: they do not reciprocate high contributions with higher own contributions, nor do they reduce their contributions further in retaliation when others defect. Their contribution is effectively insensitive to the contribution environment, except for the last round, when they suddenly match the group’s norm. The final full contribution may indicate (low‑confidence inference) either a late attempt to appear cooperative, a belief that it no longer matters strategically, or simple experimentation; the data do not clearly discriminate among these possibilities.

Overall, they exhibit low risk tolerance with respect to own payoff: they systematically choose the individually dominant strategy (keeping almost all coins) rather than the socially optimal one. Their contributions are highly stable and predictable, reflecting a pre‑set strategy more than round‑by‑round adaptation.

<COMMUNICATION>
The player is socially engaged, light‑hearted, and maintains an amiable tone even while free‑riding. They greet others, ask informational questions about the game mechanics and external survey platforms, and make playful or mildly ironic comments about the situation. They do not argue for their own contribution style, nor do they ever explicitly acknowledge that they are contributing far less than others, beyond joking references to being “consistent.”

They respond directly when other players talk about them (e.g., the “green bird” label), clarifying their identity in a humorous way rather than becoming defensive or apologetic. They do not attempt to persuade others to defect with them; if anything, they ironically echo the group’s pro‑cooperation rhetoric (“don’t let the team down”) while continuing to under‑contribute, suggesting a tendency toward teasing or trolling rather than sincere norm enforcement.

They show curiosity about both the rules (e.g., asking about the multiplier) and about side topics (survey income, bank switches), and they occasionally initiate small‑talk questions. They are responsive to chat but not to the substance of others’ strategic complaints; they do not adjust behavior when criticized. Their engagement style is conversational and somewhat playful, with mild sarcasm but no direct hostility. They also do not attempt to coordinate a coalition of defectors; they free‑ride as a lone strategy while verbally blending into the cooperative majority.

<RESPONSE_TO_OTHERS_OUTCOME>
This player clearly observes that others earn less when they under‑contribute, because the group repeatedly points out that non‑max contributors are reducing everyone’s payoffs, and their own round results are consistently higher than most others’. Despite this, there is no sign of guilt, compensatory behavior, or a shift toward fairness. They continue their low contributions through heavy social pressure, insults, and repeated explanations of the collective outcome structure.

They do not praise high contributors or sympathize with those who lose out; they largely ignore payoff disparities in their strategic choices. Their main explicit reaction related to outcomes is to report their own high cumulative total when asked, which they do matter‑of‑factly without acknowledging that this total is partly due to free‑riding. When other players frame them as the group‑project “slacker” type, they do not defend their fairness but also do not change course, indicating low sensitivity to social disapproval when it conflicts with monetary gain.

The final‑round full contribution does not come with any explicit comment linking it to others’ past outcomes, so it is unclear whether it reflects empathy, conformity, or simple experimentation; this remains unknown. Overall, they prioritize own payoff maximization and strategic detachment over aligning with group payoffs, while maintaining a socially pleasant front in chat.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a strategic, alternating cooperator–free rider who understands that full contribution by everyone yields high payoffs but is willing to exploit cooperative norms intermittently. They frequently contribute fully and participate in many rounds of full-group cooperation, but they also choose to withhold contribution in a non-random, opportunistic pattern. The withholding occurs both when others are consistently contributing and when the group shows some wavering, suggesting the player is tracking the environment but not simply matching others’ behavior.

They accept substantial benefit from others’ cooperation and show no observable guilt response in their choices afterward; they resume contributing when it is advantageous or when group cooperation is restored, not when they are called out. They demonstrate low aversion to being identified as a free rider: even after being explicitly noticed as not agreeing with the cooperative plan, they continue to mix contribution and defection. They appear comfortable tolerating some social disapproval in exchange for higher personal payoffs.

Their behavior is consistent with calculated, payoff-oriented conditional cooperation: they contribute when they expect high total contributions and occasionally “cash in” by withholding while others still contribute. Unlike pure defectors, they are willing to bear the personal short-term cost of contributing in many rounds, but unlike strong norm enforcers, they do not maintain full cooperation once the opportunity to exploit is clear. Fairness concerns toward others’ payoffs are weak or absent in their revealed choices; own payoff maximization dominates. Risk tolerance toward potential social sanction is moderate to high; there is no evidence of fear-based conformity to the cooperative chat norm.

<COMMUNICATION>
The player is minimally communicative and socially low-key. They offer only a brief greeting early on and do not re-engage in substantive discussion, even when cooperation norms and strategies are actively debated. They do not attempt to persuade, coordinate, or justify their behavior. When other players discuss contribution strategies, bonuses, or the purpose of the game, this player remains silent.

They show low responsiveness to explicit social cues. When another player publicly remarks that this player has not followed the cooperative suggestion, they do not respond verbally, either to deny, explain, or adjust their approach. This suggests a preference for quiet, non-confrontational deviation rather than open instrumental negotiation or leadership. Their style is to “fly under the radar”: they exploit the public goods structure but do not engage enough in chat to become a social focal point.

Personality-wise, they present as reserved, pragmatic, and possibly task-focused rather than socially motivated. There is no evidence of hostility or trolling; the lack of engagement looks more like disengagement from the social layer than antagonism. Their silence when others emphasize teamwork and mutual benefit indicates that social approval, group identity, or collective framing are not strong motivators for their decisions, or at least not strong enough to override their opportunistic choices.

<RESPONSE_TO_OTHERS_OUTCOME>
This player monitors others’ contributions and outcomes but uses that information primarily to calibrate their own payoff strategy rather than to enforce or support group norms. They clearly recognize that when everyone contributes, everyone earns more, as shown by their repeated willingness to contribute during stable cooperative phases. However, they also choose to free ride in precisely those cooperative contexts where doing so yields especially high personal payoffs, indicating that recognition of group benefit does not translate into consistent norm adherence.

When others earn more by defecting, there is no observable retaliatory defection pattern; their own defections do not line up cleanly as punishment responses but as independent profit-taking. When non-contributors gain extra in some rounds, the focal player does not shift into a fully defecting, downward-spiral mode, nor do they escalate; instead they continue to oscillate between contributions and free riding. This suggests they are not driven by envy or inequality aversion in a strong way—others’ advantage does not obviously trigger corrective action.

Social feedback about outcomes—such as public acknowledgement that the group can maximize payoffs by mutual contribution, or explicit noticing of this player’s non-cooperation—does not induce a sustained change in their pattern. They occasionally return to contributing after such moments, but they also later defect again despite continued cooperative talk. This implies limited sensitivity to social reputation or group approval as inferred from shared outcomes and chat.

Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is instrumental: they adjust enough to keep the group from collapsing into full defection and to continue benefiting from high-contribution states, while intermittently exploiting those states for extra gain. Moral or fairness-based interpretation of payoffs appears weak or unknown; the dominant observable driver is opportunistic earning, with low emotional or communicative reaction to distributional patterns.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a near-unconditional high contributor with a strong cooperative norm. They reliably give the maximum possible amount in almost every round and only once deviate slightly below the maximum, despite others intermittently free-riding or reducing contributions. Their behavior indicates that they prioritize group efficiency over individual short-term gain and are willing to tolerate known free-riders without retaliating by lowering their own contributions. They appear comfortable accepting some exploitation if the group outcome is good, suggesting low sensitivity to inequity in the direction of being “too generous.” There is little evidence of strategic conditional cooperation; their contributions do not systematically track others’ behavior or the game’s remaining horizon. Risk tolerance with respect to being exploited is high; they do not hedge by contributing less when some others clearly defect. Fairness norms are pro-social and group-oriented rather than focused on strict reciprocity.

<COMMUNICATION>
Their communication style is positive, cooperative, and lightly directive. Early on, they explicitly encourage everyone to contribute the maximum, framing it as a shared norm and a way to make the interaction more efficient and smoother. They combine instrumental reasoning (cooperate to increase payoffs) with process-oriented preference (cooperate to “make this go faster”), revealing that they value both outcome efficiency and minimizing friction or delay. Their tone is friendly, upbeat, and somewhat playful; they use mild humor and light self-reference to maintain a good atmosphere. They make only a few chat contributions and do not engage in arguments or calling out defectors, preferring to focus on encouragement and expressing satisfaction when the group cooperates. They respond to the general cooperative mood but do not appear highly reactive to specific individuals’ remarks, suggesting a low-conflict, facilitative engagement style rather than intense back-and-forth persuasion.

<RESPONSE_TO_END_GAME>
They show no detectable end-game defection or strategic tapering. Contributions remain maximal through the final rounds despite visible declines in group cooperation and the clear knowledge of how many rounds remain. They do not shift to self-focused extraction when the game horizon shortens, indicating either a stable internal norm (“always cooperate”) or limited concern with final-round incentives. There is no visible attempt to exploit others in the last round by free-riding after having encouraged cooperation earlier. Their end-game behavior is consistent with rule-based cooperation rather than backward-induction reasoning or payoff-maximizing defection. Any nuanced end-game beliefs beyond this (e.g., expectations about others defecting) are unknown.

<REWARD>
They never spend coins to reward others, even when many players clearly cooperate at high levels and others use the reward mechanism generously. This suggests a strong preference for cost-free cooperation over costly social enforcement or recognition. Their own cooperative behavior does elicit rewards from others, but they do not reciprocate through the reward channel, implying that they do not view monetary reward as necessary for sustaining norms in this context, or they place a relatively high subjective cost on spending coins for that purpose. They also do not use rewards tactically to steer behavior or to thank especially cooperative partners. Their strategy appears to be “cooperate directly, let others handle rewards if they wish,” rather than participating actively in the reward economy. Whether this reflects principled frugality, uncertainty about reward efficacy, or simple inattention to the mechanism is unknown.

<RESPONSE_TO_OTHERS_OUTCOME>
They observe that some players consistently keep all or most of their endowment and that others invest in rewarding, yet they do not visibly change their own behavior or comment critically. They neither retaliate against free-riders nor specially praise or publicly align with high spenders on rewards. Their chat contributions remain positive and cooperation-focused rather than outcome-focused; they acknowledge group success and express satisfaction when contributions are high but avoid direct commentary on specific payoff discrepancies. This indicates low overt concern with distributive justice at the level of messaging, and a reluctance to shame or single out defectors. They tolerate seeing others earn higher payoffs via free-riding and others reduce their payoffs via generous rewards without adjusting their own strategy, suggesting that their personal rule (“contribute fully, keep things pleasant”) is relatively fixed and not strongly contingent on the emerging payoff landscape. Internal emotional responses beyond what they state are unknown.

<CONTRIBUTION>
This player is a mostly stable, high-contribution cooperator after an initial test of defection. At the start, they take the selfish option once, then switch to always contributing the full endowment and maintain that pattern without further deviation. This suggests they quickly infer that cooperation is individually profitable under the given multiplier and/or socially desirable, and then treat full contribution as their default rule.

Their behavior appears weakly conditional: the initial free-ride followed by a shift to consistent full contribution is consistent with an early exploration of the payoff structure or others’ behavior, after which they commit to cooperation even when some group members continue to defect. They do not visibly retaliate against low contributors by lowering their own contribution, even when free-riding becomes salient and repeated, which indicates relatively low propensity for payoff-maximizing conditional cooperation in the short term and a relatively high tolerance for being exploited.

They act as if they accept inequality in outcomes: they keep contributing fully even when it is clear that some others persistently take zero-cost benefits from the public fund. This is compatible with either a strong efficiency norm (“maximize the group total even if some free-ride”) or a simple rule-based strategy (“just always give the full amount once you’ve decided it’s best”), rather than fine-tuned reciprocity. Their risk tolerance appears moderate: they are willing to incur certain short-term individual cost in the presence of uncertain group cooperation, but they do not experiment with more complex patterns (no oscillation between contribute/keep). After the initial adjustment, their strategy is simple, deterministic, and cooperation-biased.

<COMMUNICATION>
The player communicates sparsely but decisively. They speak only once in the chat, and use that single moment to advocate unequivocally for full cooperation at all decision points. The message is clear, prescriptive, and framed as an ongoing norm, not a one-off suggestion. This reflects a preference for efficiency-oriented coordination over emotional confrontation: they do not call out specific defectors, but instead broadcast a simple cooperative rule for everyone.

Their tone is directive yet not hostile. They do not engage in arguments, shaming, or back-and-forth with others, even though some players are visibly frustrated at free-riders. They neither criticize nor defend anyone by name. This suggests a low-drama, task-focused engagement style: they are willing to participate to set a norm, then largely withdraw from the social conversation.

They respond to the social environment indirectly rather than interactively. Others are already discussing contribution levels when they speak; their contribution to the discourse reinforces the cooperative side without referencing specific prior remarks. There is no visible adaptation of their wording to others’ emotions or objections. They thus appear as a quiet norm-setter: they endorse a group-beneficial standard, then rely on their own behavior to model it rather than on continued persuasion.

<RESPONSE_TO_END_GAME>
The player shows no observable end-game defection. Once they switch to full cooperation, they maintain that behavior until the final round, including the last decision when there is no future interaction to maintain. This indicates that they either (a) do not apply a backward-induction logic, (b) place little weight on short-term opportunistic gains relative to their chosen rule, or (c) hold a norm or belief that “the cooperative action is correct regardless of the round.”

They appear horizon-aware in the sense that the number of rounds is visible, yet their pattern does not change as the game approaches its conclusion. There is no cooperation decay, no attempt to “cash out” at the end, and no special communication about the final round. They show moderate risk tolerance: they sometimes contribute fully even when others have not been uniformly high contributors and there is no punishment, indicating a willingness to take short-term risks on the group. At the same time, they are comfortable with partial free-riding, which lowers their exposure to downside risk. Overall, they resemble a pragmatic conditional cooperator who uses the contribution level as a personal tuning knob rather than a moral commitment.

They do not visibly retaliate by lowering their own contribution after others free‑ride; instead they continue to cooperate and try to bring deviators back into line through communication. This suggests a strong fairness and efficiency norm, low inclination to exploit others, and relatively high tolerance for being marginally exploited if communication might fix the issue. Their risk tolerance appears moderate-to-high in social terms: they are willing to “trust” the group with continuous full contributions even after repeated defections, but they also show awareness of the payoff structure and the incentive to free‑ride.

<COMMUNICATION>
They actively engage in chat and adopt a friendly, informal, and persuasive tone. Early on they initiate coordination around full contributions, framing it as clearly optimal for everyone. When others deviate, they confront them directly but in a relatively non-aggressive way, using light pressure, reasoned arguments, and mild social labels like “snaking” to signal disapproval. They also provide simple, concrete instructions to confused players about how to choose the cooperative action, indicating a teaching/mentoring orientation. They reassure apologetic or mistaken players and quickly signal “no hard feelings” after errors, which helps maintain group cohesion. They show some responsiveness to others’ concerns (e.g., acknowledging time pressure or confusion) and repeatedly try to keep the group aligned on the cooperative strategy rather than disengaging or escalating conflict. Overall, they use communication primarily for coordination and norm enforcement via explanation and soft social pressure, not for banter or status.

<PUNISHMENT>
They rarely punish, but when they do, it is targeted and norm-based. Punishment is directed at clear free‑riding or very low contribution levels that reduce group efficiency, rather than at minor deviations or personal slights. They do not appear to punish out of spite for their own past punishment, nor do they join in group “piling on” purely for fun; instead, they ignore multiple calls from others to coordinate costful sanctions against a previously low-contributing player, even after that player has been labelled a “troll.” This indicates they view punishment as a tool for basic norm enforcement, not as entertainment or vendetta. Their behavior suggests a deterrence and correction motive (aimed at egregious under-contributors or multi-round slackers), combined with a reluctance to overspend on sanctions when contributions are currently back at the cooperative norm. They are also willing to sanction more than one deviant in a round if multiple players’ contributions fall well below the group standard, reflecting a consistent concern with fairness rather than singling out only one target.

This isolated act of punishment is low in magnitude relative to what others are spending. They do not appear to retaliate for being punished themselves, as they later receive deductions yet do not respond with any punishment behavior.  

Motivationally, their punishment use looks more deterrence- and norm-based than vengeful. Overall, they come across as a “cautious conditional cooperator with opportunistic streaks”: they value a cooperative, high-contribution environment, are minimally willing to pay once to signal norms via a small punish/reward pair, but predominantly conserve resources while letting others shoulder most of the enforcement and high-contribution burden.

They appear sensitive to perceived fairness and reciprocity. When they free-ride, it is in an environment where one other player systematically free-rides while the remaining two contribute fully. Their pattern suggests testing or expressing frustration rather than adopting a systematic exploitative strategy. There is no evidence of nuanced conditional cooperation (e.g., fine-tuning contribution levels according to others’ averages), because only extreme options are available; but within those constraints, they tend to align with a “high cooperation norm” and only depart from it episodically.

Risk tolerance seems moderate. They willingly “risk” being punished when they free-ride and accept that cost; however, they do not persistently free-ride after severe punishment, instead shifting back to full contributions. They may believe that continued high contribution improves group outcomes or maintains a cooperative standard, even if one group member persistently deviates.

Overall, their contribution “type” is: high-cooperation-norm follower with intermittent, reactive free-riding driven by fairness concerns and emotional responses, rather than by a stable payoff-maximizing defection strategy.

<PUNISHMENT>
This player is clearly willing to punish and sometimes punishes very heavily, including against players who are themselves high contributors. Their use of punishment is not limited to sanctioning the obvious free-rider; at various times they target the high contributors as well. This indicates that their punishment behavior is not simply “punish defectors, protect cooperators.”

They do not punish every round; there are long stretches of zero punishment. When they do punish, the intensity and coverage of targets shifts: sometimes they punish two high contributors strongly, sometimes just the free-rider lightly, sometimes all or nearly all others. This inconsistency suggests that punishment is driven more by transient emotions or shifting interpretations of others’ intent than by a fixed, transparent rule.

There is some indication of retaliatory or expressive motives. When they are punished for free-riding, they later engage in very heavy punishment against the same high-contributing players who had previously been natural “allies” in cooperation. The scale of this punishment is so large that it is very costly to them and not obviously strategic from a payoff-maximizing perspective. This is consistent with a desire to “hit back,” express anger, or reassert fairness norms after feeling targeted, even at significant personal cost. They also sometimes punish the persistent free-rider, but not consistently or proportionally across the interaction, which suggests their central trigger is not just contribution level.

Selectivity is low: they are willing to punish both cooperators and defectors and to escalate punishment substantially. They do not show a stable pattern of using small, targeted sanctions to shape behavior. Instead, their severe sanctions appear episodic and clustered, consistent with frustration episodes.

Deterrence motives are uncertain. Because players cannot observe who punishes and cannot see others’ final payoffs, they cannot verify whether their punishment changes others’ behavior. There is no clear evidence that this player punishes to systematically deter free-riding: the free-rider continues to free-ride, and yet the player does not adopt a consistent “always punish free-rider” strategy. Their punishment pattern fits better with emotional or norm-enforcement motives that are loosely connected to actual effectiveness.

In sum, their punishment style is: emotionally driven, sometimes vengeful, willing to incur very high personal costs, and not tightly aligned with classical altruistic punishment that cleanly tracks defection.

<REWARD>
This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite multiple opportunities, and despite repeatedly observing stable high contributions from some group members. Given that reward is relatively efficient (each unit is a net gain for the recipient), their consistent non-use suggests a strong preference against positive reinforcement as a tool.

This could reflect one or more of the following, all low confidence: a belief that rewarding is unnecessary because high contribution is the “default” expected behavior; a preference for punishing norm violations over rewarding norm adherence; or a desire to conserve coins for own payoff or for occasional punishment. However, none of these can be confirmed directly—only inferred from their complete abstention from rewarding.

They also accept rewards from others when offered, but do not reciprocate. This absence of reciprocal rewarding, even when they themselves are high contributors and benefit from others’ generosity, suggests that gratitude signaling through rewards is not part of their behavioral repertoire in this setting.

Overall, their reward “type” is: pure non-rewarder—relies exclusively on punishment and contribution choices for social control and expression, and does not allocate resources to positive incentives under any observed circumstances.
